sentences.text: Yeah.
In 19-- 1940, Warsaw-- Warsaw City was bombed.
Bricks was laid down on the street.
And there was very few building-- building standing, mostly-- mostly broken.
Then, there was a beautiful day at the time, I believe in May, June, or July.
I was arrested.
There was plenty of people in Warsaw, you know, that was walking, and the-- then the German army, SS, was walking down the street too, and somehow, so quick they come in like a convoy, SS.
Was [INAUDIBLE], I don't know what kind of [? army, ?] [INAUDIBLE].
Because in my paper, they say I was arrested by [INAUDIBLE].
And everybody, don't move, stay in your place.
And everybody arrested, children, older people, Jewish people, Jewish Polish people, Polish people, makes no difference.
No, no, no.
So that's-- that's why the first convoy, from Warsaw, 1940, they brought us straight to-- to Auschwitz.
Daytime, beautiful day.
Before-- before noon time.
Yeah.
One lady told me, she told me about [INAUDIBLE], be careful, watch-- watch because [INAUDIBLE] arrest.
I said-- I said nothing happened to me.
I just ignore them.
No, there was first massive arrest that day.
June, I believe, 12th, June or July.
Everybody, everybody, even people who even hardly could walk, older people, like-- like Polish Jewish people.
For them, there was no mercy.
On the-- no, we marched it.
We marched it.
And by the-- by night to the-- I think it was [INAUDIBLE].
It was Polish cavalry, first Polish cavalry was before the war.
They-- they hold us on the saw dust, because that was for the horses.
For three days, we're sitting, on a middle, those-- those are machine guns, and nobody could-- could move no place.
And after three days--
Maybe throw it just like, like you throw it to the zoo, to the lions or tiger.
For three days.
I believe maybe 4,000, 5,000.
Yeah.

And after three days, the-- the-- the-- I believe, if I remember, they put us on the trucks to the-- to the Praga, that's Praga that's divided, like-- like Manhattan and Brooklyn, because divided by the Vistula.
And they travel-- travel us to the station.
And I remember truck, there was truck, and an SS motorcycle with a machine gun, truck, SS with a machine.
Freight.
Everybody.
Yes.
Everybody.
It was-- like a sardine.
Yes.
No place to even-- even sit because it was so tight.
Three, or three and a half, or four days.
There was many escape, many times the train stopped.
Many-- many machine guns shooting.
Maybe, some escape.
Some were killed.
No.
I don't know.
No, there was so many people.
Because I was-- I was only one, [INAUDIBLE] [? too. ?] So nobody know me, I don't know nobody.
I know there was Polish Jewish, Polish, and--
Yes, yes.
Very sadness.
Some people knew already the [INAUDIBLE] Auschwitz.
To me, I had no knowledge, nothing.
I never even knew that Auschwitz existed.
Yeah, some people guessing, because I hear when-- when there was talking, [? the older ?] people, so we're going to Auschwitz.
Many people died, fainted.
I was very tough, very strong.
So it doesn't bother me.
Even I could stand 24 hours on own feet.
Maybe some chocolate.

I'm not sure, because usually when I was there, walking the street, there was a chunk of sugar, Polish sugar that was given for the horses, I carried [INAUDIBLE] chocolate, very-- a very famous chocolate.
I believe they give bread [BACKROUND NOISE] and maybe water.
I'm not so sure.
On the train.
To the Auschwitz.
Scared to death.
Like a-- when I watch it now on a movie, it was much worse.
No, not in 1940-- it was very few people.
And a couple thousand people in Auschwitz.
And it was very strictly, the SS was monsters.
We would be nothing.
We would be nothing [INAUDIBLE].
Mostly SS.
[INAUDIBLE] SS were there.
Dogs with a man, all kind of different gestapo, SS, and there was about three four branches of their army.
They were walking on their arms, all-- all around there by the-- by the freighter cars.
No, we been already in the-- in the train.
Yeah, two or three, small spaces, because the freighter car was not tight [INAUDIBLE] you could watch through the-- through the spaces.
I believe everybody.
Directly to Auschwitz.
No.
Went to-- very hot in Auschwitz.
People fainted.
There was not enough water to drink.
And everybody gets slapped, kick, or even killed-- pushed with a rifle to stop.
I believe, yes.
Yeah.
From Auschwitz, there was a train stop on the ramp, very high, almost one flight, the the [? freighter ?] was open on a [INAUDIBLE].
And the German, they say [SPEAKING GERMAN].
That's German.
I'll never forget it.
Those-- we had to jump it, almost one story because the ramp was so high, and we jump it.
Some people when they jump it, got broken leg.
Some people could not pick up, and several German come and kick-- kick them, and-- and hit them with a rifle.
Everybody would line up, I believe five or 10, line up, and they marched directly into Auschwitz.
Where it say ?] Arbeit Macht Frei.
Right.
Yes.
Yeah.
On a-- everybody stand on a-- on a-- in the middle of Auschwitz, on a place.
Then the [GERMAN] SS man, [GERMAN] that's like [INAUDIBLE].
[GERMAN] his name was Palitzsch, [INAUDIBLE].
He was an SS, much higher in rank.
We were scared of him to death.
When he come on a motorcycle from Auschwitz to Birkenau, and I was witness.
Four or five SS men was standing and talking between themselves.
When the-- the one of the SS say, [INAUDIBLE] Palitzsch, the SS split.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Then [INAUDIBLE] take a big log book and call each-- each name, by name.
From-- they kept everything, [INAUDIBLE], name, everything.
In Warsaw, I believe, name, when sitting in the stable place.
And then [INAUDIBLE] wrote every name to-- to they have to come to him and say I am [GERMAN].
[GERMAN] means professional.
What's your profession?
[GERMAN] They said, good.
[GERMAN], doctor, kick, slap.
[SPEAKING GERMAN] a student.
Very good.
[GERMAN], name, [GERMAN].
[GERMAN] Very good.
[GERMAN] Lawyer, attorney, slap, kick.
Mostly intelligent people being punished.
Right away.
Separated people.
Their profession.
[GERMAN], a student.
With young people.
Special barracks for the young.
Seven, number seven barrack.
Yes.
Set ?] Barracks, black, [? sadness. ?] Long hall.
And it--
There was no bunk.
You sleep on the concrete in the-- in the straw, mattress, I believe.
No, there was bricks, bricks.
Auschwitz was bricks.
At that time, there was a single building.
Then after, when the-- been work-- people would be working 24 hours, day and night, they shift.
They make it two, three story, higher.
There-- there-- there-- because in Auschwitz, there was Polish-- Polish army was in Auschwitz, those single-- single building, one story.
When we get there, there was not enough room.
There were already architect from Berlin come, they have to take off the roofs, make them two, three story higher.
One story, like it was, you know, there was-- from before the war.
On the floor.
Maybe 1,200.
We sleep like a sardine.
Yeah, I guess.
In the summer, there was-- it was very hot.
Impossible to--
No, there-- there-- our clothes take away from us.
At the beginning, we-- we be in our clothes, but when we went to take shower, I don't know when, next day maybe, they take away our clothes.
We get the prisoner clothes.
Like you saw [INAUDIBLE], blue and white, on some like that.
Stripes.
In the winter time, we got different clothes.
They were more heavy.
In the summertime, you had the more light, blue, white, a little bit lighter.
Yes, there was a cook-- cooks, there was-- there was kitchen already made.
They made us some kind of soup.
Prisoner.
There was already prisoner already.
There was first prison, there was from Krakow.
In the-- [INAUDIBLE].
They coming from [INAUDIBLE].
There a-- a couple hundred people, Polish people already from [INAUDIBLE].
We were big-- big-- were the first transport from the Warsaw.
From Krakow and [INAUDIBLE].
Because the number, there was one number, Polish man was number one.
They had 2,000.
That was-- he was before me.
Number one, number three, number four.
Because there was-- start from number one.
On the uniform, on a small cloth.
Whatever the [INAUDIBLE], and I believe Polish.
Yeah.
[INAUDIBLE] I was a political prisoner, because-- which-- I belonged to no [INAUDIBLE] organization because I was too young.
But as-- as-- to German, I was as political, because I was very active when I been in the civil life, the German put me in a camp.
They knew I was Polish student.
Then they knew everything about me.
I was-- I don't belong to any organization.
No, impossible.
Yes.
No, but my-- my parents was afraid to write to me at Auschwitz.
No.
No, I don't know.
They knew-- they were scared.
Maybe-- they [INAUDIBLE].
That's why they were scared to contact me.
From beginning, there was Polish Jewish and Polish.
Together, yes, yes.
No, no, no.
Because it was-- at the beginning, first, one, two days, there was disorganize.
I believe we went to-- we take bathtub, shower.
And I believe there was kind of separation.
As-- as-- as far as I remember, separation of Polish, Polish Jewish and Polish people.
At the beginning, they [INAUDIBLE] women, only Polish, Polish people.
Polish Jewish, Polish people.
Men.
You got me.
I know-- I know they arrested everybody, woman, not women, children, not children, but what happened with them, I don't see it.
Yeah.
Older people.
Yeah.
Yeah.
They separated-- in the beginning, there was nothing-- everybody went all through to-- to the Auschwitz.
Later, they separated, when they called each person by name.
And they have to tell [INAUDIBLE] and the profession.
Yeah, then started separating.
At the time, there was nothing, nothing.
No, not yet.
Couple days later, I believe.
By the transport serial, like the transport was 4,000.
I would say counted from one, to 2,254.
Yeah.
I believe P, a red triangle.
Yeah.
It was on-- on the-- on the left side.
Yes.
Profession-- we have all kind of people in Auschwitz, believe me.
Profession.
I remember there was-- there was not much to do.
We [INAUDIBLE] pick up the grass, where they make bricks.
On a hot, sunny day, and clean the [? alley, ?] you know, things like that.
Just given-- do-- do something, nothing.
Right.
There was two [? monsters, ?] I tell you.
They-- like I said, lager-- lageralteste, maybe familiar to you?
There was Bruno and Leo.
Bruno was short, captain from a submarine, German submarine.
Leo was about 300 or 400 pound, tall, tall man.
Monstruous. ?] Also, I believe captain from submarine.

And they hid-- hid behind the [? barrack ?] during the [INAUDIBLE] time, both of them, when they were in the concentration camp, Auschwitz, they let them go from prisoner to be [? masters ?] in the concentration camp.
They were prisoners during the [INAUDIBLE].
Those was two [? monsters, ?] Leo and Bruno.
Oh, they kill you, they do anything.
Even-- even they kill German, their friend.
They torture and [INAUDIBLE] unbelievable people.
Even I was struck a couple times by him.
And then-- and then during the lunchtime, by the-- by the [INAUDIBLE].
Because he was so powerful.
When he struck, with one [INAUDIBLE] people [INAUDIBLE] on the ground.
And I-- maybe-- maybe I annoyed him or something, that's why.
And the people, the Polish people say, [INAUDIBLE], make sure-- make sure he hits you, [INAUDIBLE] fall down.
Then I-- I realized, otherwise he'll hit me about six times, I was so strong.
Because usually when he struck someone, the men-- men fly down.
Yes, killing, like you see the baseball, they play, from the handle from a shovel, I think, people been killed.
Mostly the kapos.
These were lageralteste, lageralteste.
One, like [INAUDIBLE].
Like I say, [LAUGHS] I call him [INAUDIBLE].
Lageralteste number one, Bruno.
And lageralteste number two, Leo.
There was three rules, only was [INAUDIBLE] to SS.
Those civilian-- civilian prisoner, they-- they could do whatever they wanted.
The kapo work on the commando, on the work.
There was ?] kapo they calling.
Soon, I started to work.
[INAUDIBLE].
First I work in a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in the commando.
I-- I work in a commando, separate to the houses, because on periphery Auschwitz, I believe 40 to 60 kilometers square.
I think people who live in beautiful houses have to leave the houses, and they move out or put them in the concentration camp.
And the houses was beautiful, beautiful, beautiful.
Most of the rich people before the war lived in-- in [INAUDIBLE] Auschwitz.
I had to break it into pieces and make it even ground so-- so it could be observed-- what you call it-- make better view.
Yeah.
Yeah, that was my-- I work for the commando.
And there was, I don't know, a couple-- a couple thousand people been working at different houses, different [INAUDIBLE] place.
Every day, after that, everyday.
I hear that people come, not only from Poland, France Jewish, from France, French people, from Belgium, Holland.
Auschwitz was, I tell you, Auschwitz was very important.
I-- there was-- like-- like in a plan made already.
Auschwitz as excellent transportation from-- from entire Europe to Auschwitz.
Even from Moscow.
They made a special truck, because Moscow-- a Russian truck-- truck is much wider.
So they made it from Moscow to-- to Auschwitz.
Better connection.
Yes.
In 1940, Auschwitz was impossible to-- to be alive.
Some people, intelligent, get crazy, get emotion, not stable, mostly intelligent people, like a professor, scientist, doctor, in two or three weeks, they get really crazy.
So they don't care they-- what happened to him.
So they went on electric-- electric [? fence, ?] get-- get-- get [INAUDIBLE] get shock or get shot at by tower, by the SS men.
Especially on a holiday, the SS, [INAUDIBLE] to-- to shoot a haftling, prisoner.
So the German Wehrmacht, the German SS, got vacation, two or three weeks, or more, for that reason.
Anytime, anytime.
Yeah, we-- even the people going so close, the electric fence was so powerful, if-- if I stand that-- that close to the-- to that [INAUDIBLE], the electric pull me to the fence.
The [? electroactive ?] so powerful.
It's very difficult for me, long days.
Yes.
Sometime, we-- on the lunchtime, we come to-- to Auschwitz.
They feed us.
Sometimes when we worked far away, they-- they bring them the soup to the place where we'd been working, like a [GERMAN].
[GERMAN] been working on their [INAUDIBLE].
Different-- different places.
Auschwitz, I want you know, Auschwitz is-- was a very rich place.
Auschwitz could support themselves.
Auschwitz was very rich.
There was race horses, race pigs, a mill, flour mill in Auschwitz.
Fishing, it was-- it was really-- no, Auschwitz was absolutely independent, like New York.
In the beginning, there was like corn flakes, some kind of spinach.
In the beginning, it was different food, and later, it was very bad food.
Yeah.
